2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I clicked “Submit” on my first Google Summer of Code proposal in 2025, I genuinely believed I had a good chance.
I had been contributing to Checkstyle for a few months, had several pull requests merged, actively interacted with maintainers, and spent weeks refining my proposal after reading previous successful submissions and project documentation.
I knew the competition would be tough. But I also felt prepared. Then the results were announced.
My name wasn’t on the list.
Like many first-time applicants, I immediately started asking myself the wrong question:
“Was I not good enough?”
Looking back, I realize that wasn’t the right question at all.
The better question was:
“What was I missing?”
That shift in mindset changed everything.
What I Thought GSOC Was Looking For
When I applied for the first time, I believed three things mattered the most:

• A technically strong proposal.

• A decent number of merged pull requests.

• Good communication with maintainers.
While these are important, I later realized they are only part of the equation.
GSOC isn’t simply about proving that you can write code. It’s about demonstrating that you understand the project well enough to independently deliver a meaningful feature over several months.
There’s a big difference.
The Feedback That Changed My Perspective
One of the things I appreciated most about the Checkstyle community was the honest feedback from the maintainers.
Instead of treating rejection as the end of the conversation, they helped me understand how I could become a stronger candidate.
A few suggestions stood out:

• Contribute across different parts of the project instead of staying within a narrow area.

• Develop a deeper understanding of the repository as a whole.

• Consider multiple project ideas instead of focusing on just one.

• Continue contributing even outside the GSOC application window.
None of this was about writing better Java. It was about becoming a better contributor.
The Mistake I Didn’t Realize I Was Making
For months, my focus had quietly shifted toward getting selected.
Every contribution felt like another step toward GSOC.
After the rejection, I asked myself a simple question:
If GSOC didn’t exist, would I still contribute to this project?
Surprisingly, my answer was yes.
Somewhere along the journey, I had genuinely started enjoying open source. I enjoyed understanding unfamiliar parts of the codebase. I enjoyed reading review comments. I enjoyed improving with every pull request.
That realization completely changed my motivation.
Instead of contributing for GSOC, I started contributing because I wanted to become a better engineer.
Ironically, that mindset ended up making me a stronger GSOC candidate.
What Changed Over the Next Year
I didn’t dramatically increase the number of hours I spent contributing.
I was still balancing a full-time role, so time was always limited.
What changed was how I contributed.
Instead of looking only for beginner-friendly issues, I gradually explored different areas of the project. I spent more time understanding why certain implementation decisions were made. I paid closer attention to review comments — not just on my own pull requests, but on other contributors’ work as well.
I became more comfortable navigating unfamiliar parts of the repository instead of staying within my comfort zone.
Most importantly, I stopped measuring progress by the number of merged pull requests. I started measuring it by how much I had learned.
The Hidden Benefit Nobody Talks About
The funny thing is that the biggest reward during that year wasn’t another pull request.
It wasn’t even GSOC. It was the impact on my day job.
Working on a mature open-source project changed the way I approached engineering problems.
I became better at:

• understanding large codebases,

• debugging unfamiliar systems,

• writing maintainable code,

• reviewing changes critically,

• and communicating technical decisions clearly.
These weren’t skills I consciously set out to learn.
They were simply the by-product of contributing consistently.
A Different Mindset in 2026
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When GSOC applications opened again, I applied with a very different mindset.
Of course I wanted to be selected.
But this time, I wasn’t emotionally dependent on the outcome.
I had already gained something valuable regardless of the result.
Open source had made me a better engineer.
That realization removed a surprising amount of pressure.
I focused on writing the best proposal I could, backed by a year of deeper contributions and a much stronger understanding of the project.
This time, it worked.
If You’re Applying Next Year
If you’re reading this after receiving a rejection, here’s something I wish someone had told me:
A rejection doesn’t necessarily mean you’re not technically capable.
Sometimes it simply means you haven’t had enough time to build trust, understand the project deeply, or demonstrate the consistency that long-term open-source projects naturally value.
Those things can’t be rushed.
And that’s okay.
Because the goal isn’t just to get into GSOC.
The goal is to become the kind of engineer who naturally belongs in projects like these.
For me, that transformation didn’t happen in four months.
It happened over fourteen months of showing up consistently, learning from every review, and refusing to let one rejection define the end of my journey.
